title: Statistics Basics — Distributions, Correlation, and Hypothesis Testing
key_insight: Real data follows distributions — the shape of that distribution determines which statistical tools are appropriate. Correlation measures relationships. Hypothesis testing tells you whether an observed difference is real or just noise.
body:
## The Normal Distribution

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# Normal distribution: bell curve, defined by mean (μ) and std (σ)
# 68% of data within ±1σ, 95% within ±2σ, 99.7% within ±3σ

mu, sigma = 100, 15  # IQ scores: mean=100, stdev=15

# Generate 10,000 samples:
data = np.random.normal(mu, sigma, 10_000)

plt.hist(data, bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.7, label='Sample')
x = np.linspace(50, 150, 200)
plt.plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sigma), 'r-', label='Normal PDF')
plt.title('Normal Distribution (μ=100, σ=15)')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

# Test if your data is approximately normal:
stat, p_value = stats.normaltest(data)
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')  # high p-value → consistent with normal
```

## Correlation

```python
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Pearson correlation: linear relationship between two variables
# -1 = perfect negative, 0 = no linear relationship, +1 = perfect positive

hours_studied = [2, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 4, 6, 3]
exam_score    = [55, 62, 75, 85, 90, 92, 95, 68, 80, 60]

corr, p_value = stats.pearsonr(hours_studied, exam_score)
print(f'Correlation: {corr:.3f}, p-value: {p_value:.4f}')
# Correlation: 0.987 — very strong positive linear relationship

# Correlation matrix for multiple variables:
df = pd.DataFrame({'hours': hours_studied, 'score': exam_score,
                   'sleep': [6,7,7,8,8,9,8,6,7,6]})
print(df.corr())
sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.show()
```

## Hypothesis Testing: The t-test

```python
from scipy import stats

# Question: did a new teaching method improve exam scores?
# H₀ (null hypothesis): the means are the same
# H₁ (alternative): the new method improved scores

old_method = [72, 68, 74, 77, 70, 65, 71, 73, 69, 72]
new_method = [80, 82, 78, 85, 83, 79, 84, 81, 76, 83]

# Independent samples t-test:
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(new_method, old_method)
print(f't-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}')
print(f'p-value: {p_value:.4f}')

# Interpret:
if p_value < 0.05:  # significance level α = 0.05
    print('Reject H₀: new method is significantly better')
else:
    print('Fail to reject H₀: no significant difference')

# p-value: probability of seeing this difference (or larger) IF null is true
# p < 0.05: less than 5% chance this is just random variation
```

## Common Pitfall: Correlation ≠ Causation

```
Correlation between ice cream sales and drowning deaths: 0.85
Cause? Summer — hot weather causes BOTH.

Correlation between shoe size and reading ability in children: 0.7
Cause? Age — older children have bigger feet AND read better.

The classic tools to establish causation:
1. Randomised controlled experiment (A/B test)
2. Instrumental variables
3. Difference-in-differences
Correlation alone never proves causation.
```
common_mistakes: Reporting p < 0.05 without effect size — a p-value says if an effect exists, not how large it is. Always report mean difference + 95% CI + Cohen's d
Using Pearson correlation for non-linear relationships — Pearson only detects linear relationships. Use Spearman for monotonic non-linear, or visualise with a scatter plot
Multiple comparisons: running 20 t-tests, some will show p < 0.05 by chance alone (5% false positive rate per test). Apply Bonferroni correction
mini_exercise: Load the iris dataset (from sklearn.datasets). Compute the correlation matrix between petal length, petal width, sepal length, sepal width. Which pair has the highest correlation? Run a t-test to test if setosa vs versicolor have different mean petal lengths.
real_world_usage: Google runs 10,000+ A/B tests per year. Each test uses a t-test (or Z-test for large samples) to decide if a UI change significantly improves click-through rate. The standard at Google is p < 0.01 (not 0.05) to reduce false positives at this scale. They also compute practical significance — statistical significance alone doesn't justify shipping.
explain_back_prompt: What is a p-value and what does p < 0.05 mean? What is the difference between correlation and causation? When would you use median instead of mean to summarise a dataset?
